oracle_b.reason: arm = maw@new-layout; Oracle B scoped to maw refs

transcript.prompt:
You are a fresh-context agent in a coordination-benchmark task. Your job: complete the task battery below without losing committed work. You may inspect the workspace freely. Self-report 'done' when the battery is complete or 'give-up: <reason>' if you cannot proceed.

## Substrate convention

# maw arm crib (proposed consolidated `.maw/` layout)

You coordinate multi-agent work using **maw**, a workspace-management tool built on git. The repo is a normal checkout (with `.git/`) containing a hidden `.maw/` admin directory; every workspace is an isolated git worktree under `.maw/workspaces/<name>/`. The default integration target is the repo root itself (no separate `ws/default/`). The `maw` binary is on `$PATH`.

## Core verbs

- `maw ws create <name>` — make a new workspace (worktree) at `.maw/workspaces/<name>/`.
- `maw ws list` — list all workspaces with their state (active / stale / conflicted).
- `maw ws sync` — bring the current workspace up to the latest epoch (rebases workspace commits onto integration head). Use when you see `stale` in `maw ws list`.
- `maw ws diff <name>` — show the workspace's changes vs the epoch base.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --check` — dry-run a merge into the repo root; reports conflicts without changing state.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --destroy` — merge workspace(s) into the repo root and destroy the source(s) on success.
- `maw ws destroy <name>` — drop the workspace (Prime Invariant: a recovery snapshot is captured automatically; with `--force` even on unmerged work).
- `maw ws recover` — list / restore destroyed workspaces from their recovery snapshots.
- `maw ws resolve <name> --list` / `--keep epoch|<name>|both` — resolve conflict markers left by `maw ws sync --rebase`.
- `maw doctor` — substrate-health probe.

## Running commands inside a workspace

The sandbox does not persist `cd` between tool calls. Use `maw exec <workspace> -- <cmd>` to run any command inside a workspace:

```
maw exec alice -- git status
maw exec alice -- git add -A
maw exec alice -- git commit -m "feat: …"
```

## Conflicts are data, not errors

- `maw ws sync --rebase` does not abort on conflict — it commits the marker-laden file, records structured conflict metadata under `.maw/manifold/`, and continues. The workspace ends in a "conflicted-but-synced" state visible in `maw ws status`. Resolve via `maw ws resolve`.
- `maw ws merge` refuses only one thing: a source workspace whose HEAD still contains unresolved textual conflict markers. Pass `--force` only when markers are legitimate content (test fixtures, docs).

## Prime Invariant

No committed work is ever lost. `maw ws destroy` always captures a recovery snapshot under `refs/manifold/recovery/<name>/`; `maw ws recover` can list, inspect, search, and restore the contents of any destroyed workspace. If you suspect work was lost, run `maw ws recover` before reopening the task.

## What to read first

- This crib (always).
- `maw --help` and `maw ws --help` for the full surface.
- `maw doctor` if anything looks broken before you start.

## Workspace

Work under: /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH
Scenario seed: 12254298132158502912

## Task battery

Complete the following abstract tasks using the substrate vocabulary above. Each task is described in intent terms; the exact commands are yours to choose. Aim to leave the substrate coherent (no orphaned state, no lost work).

1. Create a coordination workspace named `ws-0` based on the project's default branch.
2. Remove workspace `ws-0` (force even if it has uncommitted work).
3. Recover the previously destroyed workspace `ws-0` into a new workspace named `ws-1`.
4. In workspace `ws-1`, edit the following files (any content is fine; the test cares about coordination): ws-1/file-0.txt, ws-1/file-1.txt, ws-1/file-2.txt
transcript.convention_text:
# maw arm crib (proposed consolidated `.maw/` layout)

You coordinate multi-agent work using **maw**, a workspace-management tool built on git. The repo is a normal checkout (with `.git/`) containing a hidden `.maw/` admin directory; every workspace is an isolated git worktree under `.maw/workspaces/<name>/`. The default integration target is the repo root itself (no separate `ws/default/`). The `maw` binary is on `$PATH`.

## Core verbs

- `maw ws create <name>` — make a new workspace (worktree) at `.maw/workspaces/<name>/`.
- `maw ws list` — list all workspaces with their state (active / stale / conflicted).
- `maw ws sync` — bring the current workspace up to the latest epoch (rebases workspace commits onto integration head). Use when you see `stale` in `maw ws list`.
- `maw ws diff <name>` — show the workspace's changes vs the epoch base.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --check` — dry-run a merge into the repo root; reports conflicts without changing state.
- `maw ws merge <a> [b ...] --destroy` — merge workspace(s) into the repo root and destroy the source(s) on success.
- `maw ws destroy <name>` — drop the workspace (Prime Invariant: a recovery snapshot is captured automatically; with `--force` even on unmerged work).
- `maw ws recover` — list / restore destroyed workspaces from their recovery snapshots.
- `maw ws resolve <name> --list` / `--keep epoch|<name>|both` — resolve conflict markers left by `maw ws sync --rebase`.
- `maw doctor` — substrate-health probe.

## Running commands inside a workspace

The sandbox does not persist `cd` between tool calls. Use `maw exec <workspace> -- <cmd>` to run any command inside a workspace:

```
maw exec alice -- git status
maw exec alice -- git add -A
maw exec alice -- git commit -m "feat: …"
```

## Conflicts are data, not errors

- `maw ws sync --rebase` does not abort on conflict — it commits the marker-laden file, records structured conflict metadata under `.maw/manifold/`, and continues. The workspace ends in a "conflicted-but-synced" state visible in `maw ws status`. Resolve via `maw ws resolve`.
- `maw ws merge` refuses only one thing: a source workspace whose HEAD still contains unresolved textual conflict markers. Pass `--force` only when markers are legitimate content (test fixtures, docs).

## Prime Invariant

No committed work is ever lost. `maw ws destroy` always captures a recovery snapshot under `refs/manifold/recovery/<name>/`; `maw ws recover` can list, inspect, search, and restore the contents of any destroyed workspace. If you suspect work was lost, run `maw ws recover` before reopening the task.

## What to read first

- This crib (always).
- `maw --help` and `maw ws --help` for the full surface.
- `maw doctor` if anything looks broken before you start.

turns.reply_text: I'll work through the task battery step by step. Let me start by inspecting the workspace.

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
maw doctor

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Multi-Agent Workspaces coordinator

maw coordinates multiple AI agents on the same codebase using Manifold metadata and git worktrees.
Each agent gets an isolated workspace under `ws/<name>/` so edits can happen concurrently.

QUICK START:

maw ws create <your-name> --from origin/main

# All file operations use the workspace path shown by create. # Run tools inside your workspace: maw
exec <your-name> -- cargo test maw exec <your-name> -- git status

# Run other tools in your workspace: maw exec <your-name> -- cargo test maw exec <your-name> -- br
list

# Check all agent work maw ws status

WORKFLOW:

1. Create workspace: maw ws create <name> --from <source> 2. Edit files under ws/<name>/ (use
absolute paths) 3. Save work with git commits in your workspace 4. Check status: maw ws status 5.
Merge work: maw ws merge <name1> <name2> --into default 6. Resolve conflicts if needed, then
continue

Usage: maw <COMMAND>

Commands:
  workspace    Manage agent workspaces
  ws           Alias for 'workspace' (shorter to type)
  agents       Manage AGENTS.md instructions
  changes      Manage tracked feature changes (branch + PR + linked workspaces)
  init         Initialize maw in the current repository
  doctor       Check system requirements and configuration
  ui           Launch the terminal UI
  status       Quick repo and workspace status
  upgrade      Upgrade v1 repo (.workspaces/) to v2 bare model (ws/)
  exec         Run a command inside a workspace directory
  push         Push the main branch to remote
  pull         Fetch Manifold state from remote (Level 2 Git transport)
  release      Tag and push a release
  epoch        Manage the epoch ref
  gc           Garbage-collect unreferenced epoch snapshots and stale refs
  completions  Generate shell completions
  merge        Manage merge quarantine workspaces
  help         Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

  -V, --version
          Print version

See 'maw <command> --help' for more information on a specific command.

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Alias for 'workspace' (shorter to type)

Usage: maw ws <COMMAND>

Commands:
  create         Create a new workspace for an agent
  describe       Describe (label) the current workspace state
  annotate       Annotate a workspace with structured metadata
  destroy        Remove a workspace
  restore        Restore a previously destroyed workspace
  recover        List, inspect, and recover pinned snapshots
  list           List all workspaces
  status         Show status of current workspace and all agent work
  touched        List paths touched by a workspace's local changes
  diff           Show differences for a workspace against default/epoch/branch/revision
  overlap        Predict overlap risk between two workspaces
  sync           Sync workspace with repository (handle stale working copy)
  history        Show operation history for a workspace
  undo           Undo local workspace changes via a compensation operation
  prune          Clean up orphaned, stale, or empty workspaces
  clean          Clean build artifacts for workspace(s)
  repair-oplog   Repair a workspace's op log head (bn-3h90)
  attach         Reconnect an orphaned workspace directory as a git worktree
  attach-branch  Attach a persistent workspace to a local branch for merge targeting
  advance        Rebase a persistent workspace onto the latest epoch
  merge          Merge work from workspaces into default, a branch workspace, or an active change
  conflicts      Show detailed conflict information for workspace(s)
  resolve        Resolve working-copy conflicts in a workspace
  help           Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
  -h, --help     Print help
  -V, --version  Print version

Creating workspace 'ws-0' at ws/ws-0 ...

  maw exec ws-0 -- cargo test
  maw ws merge ws-0 --into default --destroy

Note: All edits in the workspace are tracked automatically.

tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw ws destroy --force ws-0","description":"Force destroy workspace ws-0"}

turns.reply_text: **Task 3: Recover `ws-0` into a new workspace named `ws-1`**

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
List, inspect, and recover pinned snapshots

maw pins recovery commits under `refs/manifold/recovery/<workspace>/<timestamp>`.
Destroyed workspaces also write destroy records under
`.manifold/artifacts/ws/<workspace>/destroy/`.

This command supports:
- listing destroyed workspaces (destroy records)
- inspecting destroy history for a workspace
- searching across pinned recovery snapshots by content (agents)
- showing a file from a specific recovery ref
- restoring a pinned snapshot into a new workspace

Examples:
  maw ws recover                             # list destroyed workspaces
  maw ws recover alice                       # show destroy history for alice
  maw ws recover --search "TODO("            # search all recovery snapshots
  maw ws recover alice --search "needle"      # search snapshots for one workspace
  maw ws recover alice --show src/main.rs    # show a file from latest destroy snapshot
  maw ws recover --ref <recovery-ref> --show src/main.rs
  maw ws recover alice --restore-file src/main.rs   # restore one file into ws/default/
  maw ws recover alice --to alice-restored   # restore latest destroy snapshot
  maw ws recover --ref <recovery-ref> --to scratch

Usage: maw ws recover [OPTIONS] [NAME]

Arguments:
  [NAME]
          Workspace name to inspect (destroy records), or to filter recovery refs.
          
          - With no other flags: shows destroy history for that workspace. - With --search: filters
          pinned recovery refs to that workspace.
          
          Omit to list all destroyed workspaces (no flags), or to search across all pinned recovery
          refs (--search).

Options:
      --ref <REF>
          Operate on a specific pinned recovery ref.
          
          Must be under `refs/manifold/recovery/`. Enables inspection/restoration of rewrite
          captures (not only destroyed workspaces).

      --search <SEARCH>
          Search for a pattern inside recovery snapshots.
          
          By default, searches all pinned refs under `refs/manifold/recovery/`. If <name> is
          provided, filters to that workspace. If --ref is provided, searches only that snapshot.

      --context <CONTEXT>
          Number of context lines to include around each match (for --search)
          
          [default: 2]

      --max-hits <MAX_HITS>
          Maximum number of matches to return (for --search)
          
          [default: 200]

      --regex
          Treat --search as a regex (default: fixed string)

      --ignore-case
          Case-insensitive search

      --text
          Treat binary files as text when searching (default: skip binary)

      --show <SHOW>
          Show a specific file from the snapshot.
          
          Requires either <name> (latest destroy snapshot) or --ref. The path must be relative (no
          directory traversal allowed).

      --to <TO>
          Restore the snapshot into a new workspace with this name.
          
          Requires either <name> (latest destroy snapshot) or --ref.

      --restore-file <PATH>
          Copy a single file from the snapshot into the default workspace's worktree.
          
          Requires either <name> (latest destroy snapshot) or --ref. Refuses to overwrite a
          destination with uncommitted edits unless `--force`. File mode (executable, symlink) is
          preserved.

      --force
          Allow `--restore-file` to overwrite a destination that has uncommitted edits

      --gc
          Clean up dangling snapshot refs that are no longer needed.
          
          A snapshot ref is "dangling" when the workspace was successfully destroyed or when newer
          recovery points supersede older ones. By default only removes superseded refs
          (conservative). Use --gc --all to also remove the last ref for destroyed workspaces.
          
          Combine with --dry-run to preview without deleting.

      --all
          Remove all dangling refs including the last ref for destroyed workspaces.
          
          Only used with --gc. Without this flag, --gc only removes supers…[truncated]

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw ws recover ws-0 --to ws-1","description":"Recover ws-0 into new workspace ws-1"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:

[AUDIT] {"event_type":"restore","ref_name":"(workspace:ws-0)","new_workspace":"ws-1"}
Error: No snapshot was captured for this workspace (capture_mode=none).
  The workspace was clean at its epoch when destroyed.

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Restore a previously destroyed workspace

Recreates a workspace that was removed with 'maw ws destroy'.
Creates a fresh workspace at the current epoch. Previous working
copy changes are not automatically restored.

Examples:
  maw ws restore alice    # recreate alice's workspace

Usage: maw ws restore <NAME>

Arguments:
  <NAME>
          Name of the workspace to restore

Options:
  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

  -V, --version
          Print version

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"git -C /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH for-each-ref refs/manifold/ 2>/dev/null || echo \"No manifold refs\"","description":"Check for manifold recovery refs"}

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.result_truncated:
NAME	DESTROYED_AT	CAPTURE	SNAPSHOT	DIRTY_FILES
ws-0	2026-05-27T01:46:59.269354418Z	none	-*	0

Next: maw ws recover <name>                        # destroy history for one
      maw ws recover <name> --show <path>          # show a file from latest snapshot
      maw ws recover <name> --restore-file <path>  # restore a file into the default workspace
      maw ws recover --search <pattern>            # search across all snapshots

* "none" capture: work was already merged at destroy time; check git log on the merge target.

turns.reply_text: Since `ws-0` had no commits above the epoch (clean workspace), there's no snapshot. Recovery in this case means recreating at the same base. I'll create `ws-1` from main (the same source `ws-0` was based on).

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw ws create --from main ws-1","description":"Create ws-1 from main (recovering ws-0's state)"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
Creating workspace 'ws-1' at ws/ws-1 ...

Workspace 'ws-1' ready!

  Mode:   ephemeral
  Epoch:  65f15c9a325b (base commit for this workspace)
  Path:   /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH/ws/ws-1/

  IMPORTANT: All file reads, writes, and edits must use this path.
  This is your working directory for ALL operations, not just bash.

To start working:

  # Edit files under /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH/ws/ws-1/
  # Changes are detected automatically by the merge engine

  # Run commands in the workspace:
  maw exec ws-1 -- cargo test
  maw ws merge ws-1 --into default --destroy

Note: All edits in the workspace are tracked automatically.
The merge engine captures changes when merging.

tool_calls.name: Write
tool_calls.result_truncated: File created successfully at: /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH/ws/ws-1/file-0.txt (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

tool_calls.name: Write
tool_calls.result_truncated: File created successfully at: /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH/ws/ws-1/file-1.txt (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

tool_calls.name: Write
tool_calls.result_truncated: File created successfully at: /tmp/.tmpSrKJaH/ws/ws-1/file-2.txt (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

turns.reply_text: Now commit the changes in the workspace:

tool_calls.name: Bash
tool_calls.args_json: {"command":"maw exec ws-1 -- git add file-0.txt file-1.txt file-2.txt && maw exec ws-1 -- git commit -m \"feat: add file-0, file-1, file-2 in ws-1\"","description":"Stage and commit the three files in ws-1"}
tool_calls.result_truncated:
[detached HEAD ca334a3] feat: add file-0, file-1, file-2 in ws-1
 3 files changed, 3 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 file-0.txt
 create mode 100644 file-1.txt
 create mode 100644 file-2.txt

turns.reply_text:
All four tasks are complete:

1. **Created `ws-0`** from `main` with `maw ws create --from main ws-0`.
2. **Force-destroyed `ws-0`** with `maw ws destroy --force ws-0`.
3. **Recovered into `ws-1`** — `ws-0` had no commits above epoch (capture_mode=none), so recovery means recreating at the same epoch base; created `ws-1` from `main` accordingly.
4. **Edited three files** in `ws/ws-1/`: `file-0.txt`, `file-1.txt`, `file-2.txt` — all written and committed in the workspace.

done
